item.title: Images of Note: Good Girl
item.description:
Count Binface: The Joke Candidate who had good policies.
Mood
Capycult. Capycult. Capycult. Capycult. Capycult. Capycult. Capycult.
it can still be one :)
Now I wanna listen to this album
Jax deserves this.
Skull Caverns, 20% mining, 80% fighting these fuckers.
Mood
This is basically the discord.
AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA
Well that goes hard
http://twitch.tv/abbiistabbii let's talk about breeding tractors.
I mean
We basically have turned Stardew into one big Mr Hands Reference on the stream
They were screaming while doing it.
The answer is Yes.
For Chats: http://discord.abbii.gay For Shitposts: http://tumblr.abbii.gay For srs and maybe shitposts: http://mastodon.abbii.gay For STREAM: http://twitch.tv/abbiistabbii

item.title: I deal in moss and moss accessories
item.description:
Are you incapable of keeping even weeds alive, but you still want some green for your home? Are you looking for a relaxing, creative hobby that's cheap and ideal for gift-giving? Thinking of filling your shelves with dozens of mini biomes until it becomes a Problem? Did you see a post about David Latimer's 60 year old enclosed ecosystem and thought "How do I get in on that"? Then Mossariums are for you!
Mosses are ancient, non-vascular plants that don't have a traditional root system. They show up in almost any biome, including urban, and are extremely hardy. Since they lack any traditional roots, they don't need any soil substrate and are happy attaching themselves to rocks, brick, sticks, bark, and many other surfaces as long as they have access to moisture to take up via their leaf surfaces. As such, mosses are pretty easy about where they are placed (for example - a glass jar) as long as the humidity and light is right.
Making your own mossarium is relatively easy, and can be done very cheaply. The actual construction process basics are too lengthy to cover here, but TerrariumTribe has an excellent guide: https://terrariumtribe.com/diy-terrarium-guide/ Most of the supplies can be gotten in pet shops that deal with reptiles and/or pet fish: * reptile/amfibian for coco fiber and dried sphagnum * aquarium for gravel, hardscape Both of those will usually also carry hardscape elements like driftwood, sticks, rocks, etc. You do not need any special tools, but I would still recommend investing in a set of long tweezers for your own sanity - or get real good at using chopsticks.
Alternatively you can of course make a fully native mossarium using only harvested soil, rocks and moss. Take note that live soil can come with surprises like fungal spores (spontaneous shrooms!) or hitchikers in the form of bugs and slugs. Bugs are generally not an issue, slugs however will happily eat your mossarium clean. Bugs in the mossarium provide a fascinating and personal look into their behavior, and made me appreciate many of them a lot more.
Springtails are the secret ingredient to a long-lasting mossarium. These tiny bugs are detritivores that keep your mossarium clean of fungus and dead leaves. They jump around like fleas when agitated but otherwise have zero reason to leave the utopia you've provided them with, so they can be added to even "open" mossariums. You can find them in pet shops where they're sold as feeder insects for pet frogs. Once you have one box you can create a farm for them - add some moist charcoal briquettes or hydropellets in a plastic container with a slice of any vegetable or fruit and you'll never want for springtails again. To add them to a mossarium, just shake them off one of the pellets/charcoal bricks!
Isopods are another common mossarium fauna, fulfilling a similar role to springtails. One caveat is that given the option, some isopod species will happily graze on your moss and other plants instead of the rotten wood you've helpfully provided them with. They are, however, much easier to find and harvest from the wild than springtails.
Of course you don't have to stick to moss exclusively. Many widely available plants thrive in the humid enclosed environment of a mossarium: * Coffee (Coffea Arabica) * Ivies (Hedera) * Coleus * Tradescantia * Fittonia * Peperomia * Asparagus (and other) ferns * Syngoniums * "Polka Dot plants" Many other tropical plants will also do well, but the above are some of the most common that are also widely available in "baby" or "mini" format. Personally I love the peperomia - there are a ludicrous amount of varieties of leaf shape but practically all of them do extremely well in a mossarium! Do NOT use succulents in a mossarium. Succulents do not do well in humid environments, and unlike moss, will absolutely not endure water on their leaves.
As for what to use as a container - whatever you want! Any clear container of any size and shape will technically work, but I would recommend NOT going for the classic brewing flask / "dame jeanne" shape as your first project. Narrow literal bottlenecks can be extremely frustrating to work with, so stick to more "reasonable" shapes with wider openings. Thrift stores and secondhand shops will often have glass vases or old glass bowls that work great if you can find a fitting lid! Often you will have to fashion your own lid and its a case of "Things fitting into Things", but some ideas for where you can find glass lids: glass plates, cheese covers, ethanol candles, candy bowls, ashtrays, etc
While doable, I would not go with any container smaller than 1L. The smaller the environment the less "buffer" you have for any swings in the ecosystem, and it becomes trickier to get a good aesthetic going. That is not to say without some effort you can't make tiny jars like this look great for Secret Santa gifts!
Mold is a real issue in mossariums. Springtails will do a lot of cleanup, but wood and high humidity is asking for problems. In my experience no amount of sterilizing, boiling, or microwaving will eleminate all fungal spores and you will almost always see some kind of mold or fungal bloom in the first month or two after your mossarium settles. Practically speaking, it's not an issue - the mold will not harm living plants, and the springtails will eventually get rid of most of it. The mold also helps decompose leaf litter! Aesthetically, some mold can discolour wooden hardscape pieces which you may not want. If it really bothers you, you can dab the affected areas with a mild vinegar/water solution.
Maintaining your mossarium is easy - provided you remembered to remove all the slugs. I told you about the slugs! Like any plants, moss need water and light. Moss has no roots, so just having a high humidity environment is all they want. Ideal humidity is anywhere between 60 - 80%. If you dont want to break out a humidity sensor, simply watch your jar. If it's always condensated - it's too moist. If it's never condensated - probably too dry, touch the moss and if need be give it a mist. If it's only condensated in the morning or evening it's basically ideal. Lightwise, moss tends to grow in shadier areas. NEVER put a mossarium in direct sunlight, not only do most mosses not endure direct sun for long, an enclosed glass environment + sun is going to slowcook your plants into soup. Put them in brighter areas of the house, or give them some artificial lighting. Most of my jars are in an indirectly lit living room with some 3W cabinet lighting leds above them, and they do fine.
If you stare too deep into the hobby, the hobby stares into you. The rest of this post is just some of the jars I've created over the past years, I hope I can inspire you to make mossy pots of your own and you too become incapable of looking at glass containers without thinking "I bet that would look great with some moss in it".
Another tiny jar, using a 1.2L food container from IKEA
Ficus pumilia grows like crazy and any cuttings can be made into new plants for new mossariums!
Spontaneous shrooms are a happy coincidence when using harvested wood and soil
My most recent one, using a piece of Mopani wood I've had in my drawer for over a year now.
Comission work using a candy bowl, a broken buddha ornament, and a piece of driftwood
Tall jars can be great exposition for pieces of Mopani wood or driftwood
Another comission piece, this time for a coworker. Been sitting in an enclosed fluorescent-light-only room for over a year now and is thriving.
First attempt at a custom enclosure using an old wooden bucket that I waterproofed
item.dc:creator: breadedfishstrip

item.title: I hope this helps at least one person
item.description: Tl;;d : Ideally, meet up with at least one co-worker 30 minutes before the dinner. Show up 5-10 minutes late. Sit near your boss. Engage with the server. Take the lead on what to order from the person paying the check (not necessarily the boss). Leave at just the right time. If there is even just one person that you feel a slight connection to, invite them to meet up a half hour before the dinner for a drink. It could be coffee or alcohol or just to try an appetizer at a nearby place. The important thing is that you walk in with an ally. Someone to chat with and deflect from your insecurities. If this scenario isn't possible, the rest of my advice still stands. Arrive 5-10 minutes late. I know this feels counter intuitive but, unless you're all coming from the same meeting, there will likely be a 30 minute gap from the first to the last person. The people that show up early will likely be together in their own conversation. 10 minutes later there will be a couple other groups that will take all the pressure off you. Sit near your boss. Not next to them, not directly across from them. Just within earshot. Ideally you'll be on the opposite side of the table a few seats over. You don't need to engage, in fact, unless you're incredibly confident and ambitious, I don't recommend it at all. That spot will give you both face time and an insight into how your boss and their right hands think and operate. Engage with the server. As someone who's done hundreds of these corporate parties, I feel just as ignored as you. I'm thrilled if you want to just ask me a bunch of random questions. It helps us both. You get an upper hand on any highlights or quirks about the menu and you're not just sitting there silently alone. For me, I get to feel like at least someone is paying attention to me and it prompts the other oblivious people to start focusing. Here's another little side benefit for you that I totally don't mind. When I approach a business dinner like this I rarely know who is paying. I tend to take my cue from whomever is engaging with me the most. After that point my best guess is that you'll be paying so I'm gonna take extra special care of you. Take your cue from the person paying the bill, not the boss. If your boss orders a club soda and the CFO orders an Old Fashioned, get yourself a cocktail. On the other hand, if the boss orders a cocktail and the CFO orders a Coke, get a soft drink. That goes the same for soup or salad, steak and lobster, and dessert and after dinner drinks. Just spend a little less than the one paying the bill. Deciding when to leave is a little tricky. If you are engaged in good conversations or making in roads with your higher ups, stay for a bit. If you have been watching the clock and can't wait to get out of there, try to have the patience for the first 50% of the table to leave. It will be smooth and natural and nobody will make a fuss. If you do stick around, leave before the people above you that you respect leave. I've learned not to answer comments right away because it looks like I'm double dipping. After 24 hours I will answer any questions or address any comments or concerns. Thanks for your patience.
item.dc:creator: meme2theextreme
